metadata.summary: Measure and optimize viral coefficient K = i * c; track invites per user and conversion rate separately; benchmark by product type and project exponential growth.

content.title: Viral Metrics and K-factor
content.summary: The viral coefficient K = i * c, where i is average invites sent per user and c is the conversion rate of those invites. K greater than 1 produces exponential growth; K = 0.5-1.0 amplifies other channels. Measure i, c, and viral cycle time weekly; optimize both independently.

why.p: Paid acquisition costs compound; viral growth does not. K-factor quantifies self-sustaining growth: each user with K=1.2 brings 1.2 more users, doubling the cohort every cycle. Most products target K=0.3-0.7 as a meaningful supplement to paid channels — K greater than 1 is rare and reserved for inherently collaborative or content-based products.

list.item: Designing or auditing a referral/sharing mechanism for an existing product.
Building a weekly viral dashboard to track i, c, and K by loop type and channel.
Projecting growth to determine whether viral alone can hit a user target within N cycles.
Identifying which factor (i or c) has the most improvement headroom.
Pre-retention: if D30 retention is below 20%, increasing K just cycles more users through a leaky bucket.
B2B enterprise with 6-month sales cycles — K compounds too slowly to matter for pipeline.
When invite event tracking is not yet in place — computing K from incomplete data is misleading.
Regulated products where incentivized referrals trigger disclosure requirements.

section.name: K-factor Formula and Growth Projection

rule.statement: Compute K = i * c, where i = total invites / active referrers and c = referral signups / total invites sent. Track both factors separately so you know which to optimize.
rule.rationale: K conflates two independent levers. If K drops, diagnosing whether i or c fell determines the correct fix (UX change for i, landing page for c).

example.title: K-factor Computation
example.code: Active referrers: 850 Invites sent: 4,200 -> i = 4,200 / 850 = 4.94 Referral signups: 630 -> c = 630 / 4,200 = 0.15 K = 4.94 * 0.15 = 0.74 (near-viral, strong amplifier)

section.p: K interpretation: K less than 0.5 is weak virality — supplement only, not a growth strategy. K = 0.5-1.0 is a good amplifier — each cohort grows 50-100% via referrals. K = 1.0 is break-even — each user replaces themselves. K greater than 1.0 is viral — exponential growth without ad spend. K greater than 2.0 is highly viral — rare; Slack, WhatsApp, early Wordle territory.

rule.statement: For B2B SaaS target K=0.1-0.3 baseline, K=0.5+ as "great". For consumer apps K=0.2-0.5 baseline, K=1.0+ as "great". Do not benchmark against outliers.
rule.rationale: Most benchmarks are survivorship-biased toward exceptional cases. Realistic targets by product type avoid chasing K greater than 1 at the expense of other channels.

rule.statement: Optimize viral cycle time (days from signup to successful referral conversion) as aggressively as K — halving cycle time approximately equals doubling K in compounding terms.
rule.rationale: K amplifies per cycle, not per day. A K=0.6 product with a 3-day cycle outgrows a K=0.8 product with a 14-day cycle over 60 days.

section.name: Improving i and c — Viral Optimization Levers

list.item: Natural share moments: prompt sharing at the instant of an achievement or result — not on first login
Referral incentives: reward referrers; double-sided (both parties rewarded) converts better than single-sided
Multi-channel sharing: email, SMS, copy-link, social — users have channel preferences
Contact import: allow bulk invite from address book to lower per-invite friction
Viral content: shareable artifacts (results page, badge, score) that embed product branding
Team/collaboration features: product that requires others to use inherently drives invites
Personal invitation copy: "John invited you to [Product]" vs generic blast — high impact
Social proof: "5 of your colleagues already use this" — high impact
Clear value proposition on landing page: one sentence, no jargon
Low-friction signup: one-click social auth, minimal fields — high impact
Incentive for invitee: "$10 when you join" — medium impact, verify margin allows it
Preview of value before signup: show what they will get, not just marketing copy
Track: invite_sent event with loop_type and share_channel properties
Track: invite_clicked, referral_signup, referral_activated events
Compute i, c, K weekly; flag K drops greater than 20% week-over-week
Segment K by acquisition channel and user cohort to find viral power users
Do not report K computed on fewer than 500 invites — noise dominates at small samples

antipattern.description: Over-incentivizing referrals (50%+ reward to hit K=1).
antipattern.reason: Attracts fraud and low-quality users who joined for the reward. Sets unsustainable cost expectations. K plateaus quickly; agents optimizing reward size erode margin without meaningful K lift.

antipattern.description: Using a single invite channel (email only).
antipattern.reason: Users have strong channel preferences. Email-only sharing misses SMS and social invites that convert at 25% vs email's 12-15% in many consumer apps.

antipattern.description: Sending generic invite blasts from the product (outbreak/spam virality).
antipattern.reason: Modern email filters and OS protections block automated contact imports. Damages brand NPS and can trigger CAN-SPAM violations.
